_meta.purpose: Brand-name and synonym dictionary for the compound crosswalk. Whel's compound list contains entries written as brand strings ('Wellbutrin'), as INN/USAN variants ('Paracetamol' vs 'Acetaminophen'), as salt forms ('Clomiphene Citrate'), as formulation/route descriptors ('Testosterone (transdermal)'), or as multi-ingredient combo strings ('Drospirenone/Ethinyl Estradiol (24/4 Regimen)'). The MATRIX drug-list keys by a single canonical name, so each of these forms needs an explicit translation step before crosswalking. This file lists every translation the audit script knows about, with provenance. Surfaced on /about/external-references so users can audit which translations the crosswalk applies. The `kind` field distinguishes brand (proprietary trade name), inn_variant (alternate INN/USAN spelling), abbreviation (acronym), salt_form (generic + salt suffix), formulation_variant (generic + route/form qualifier), and combo (multi-ingredient product mapped to its primary component).
_meta.scope_note: Tightly scoped. Entries fall into two buckets. (1) Reactive: every entry was a real Whel compound string that failed the name-only crosswalk and whose underlying generic IS in MATRIX. The audit's matched_via_brand_dict count reflects these rescues. (2) Proactive: alternate INN spellings of generics already in MATRIX (e.g. paracetamol -> acetaminophen, norethisterone -> norethindrone), added to keep future Whel rows from re-introducing known crosswalk gaps. Do NOT add entries for class labels, supplements, vitamins, or experimental compounds outside MATRIX -- those remain on the is_class_label / is_non_drug exclusion paths in scripts/check-matrix-coverage.py. Do NOT add misspellings; ambiguous lookups are a false-positive risk.

entries.note: FDA-approved 1985; antidepressant repurposed for smoking cessation. Brand widely used as shorthand.
FDA-approved May 2023 for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms in menopause. NK3R antagonist.
SNRI; relevant to chronic pelvic pain repurposing.
Guanylate cyclase-C agonist; appears in endometriosis-adjacent GI repurposing.
Benzodiazepine; rectal suppository formulation appears in pelvic-floor dysfunction protocols.
Brand for the LNG-IUS. Whel's compound list separately enumerates the IUS as a class umbrella; this entry covers the brand-only mentions.
Injectable contraceptive; appears in heavy-menstrual-bleeding and endometriosis literature.
Contraceptive implant; relevant to menstrual-pattern repurposing.
Pelvic-floor injection use in chronic pelvic pain. Whel's existing onabotulinumtoxinA entry is matched; this covers the brand-only row.
GLP-1 agonist; appears in PCOS and metabolic repurposing. Generic semaglutide is already matched in the crosswalk; this entry exists for transparency about which brand strings the audit recognises.
Antihistamine; appears in mast-cell-activation repurposing for endometriosis.
Antihistamine; appears in mast-cell-activation literature.

Combo product for uterine fibroids and endometriosis pain; this entry matches on relugolix as the GnRH antagonist component. Estradiol and norethindrone components are handled separately in the SINGLE candidate list.

Medical-device combo product, not a drug in DrugBank. Listed here for documentation only -- this entry will NOT match a DrugBank ID and the underlying compound remains in the experimental / device category.

Combined oral contraceptive on the 24/4 dosing regimen (e.g. Yaz). Maps to drospirenone as the progestin component; ethinyl estradiol is its own DrugBank entry (DB00977) and would be matched separately if Whel ever listed it on its own.
Generic combined oral contraceptive (no fixed regimen). Maps to drospirenone as the progestin component; the concatenated 'Ethinylestradiol' spelling (no space) is a frequent European variant.
Photosensitiser used in endometriosis lesion ablation research. Whel's row carries the delivery modality (PDT) in the string; the underlying drug is aminolevulinic acid, DrugBank DB00855.
Topical anaesthetic in vulvodynia and provoked vestibulodynia protocols. The MATRIX drug-list keys on lidocaine; the parenthetical delivery descriptor here blocks the direct lookup.
Off-label compounded dosing (typically 1.5-4.5 mg/day) of naltrexone, used in endometriosis-pain and fibromyalgia literature. MATRIX keys on naltrexone.
Oral micronized progesterone (e.g. Prometrium) used in HRT and luteal-phase support. MATRIX keys on progesterone.
Bioidentical / compounded progesterone formulations referenced in perimenopause and PMDD literature. MATRIX keys on progesterone.
Transdermal testosterone used in HSDD (hypoactive sexual desire disorder) and perimenopause libido protocols. MATRIX keys on testosterone.
Topical compounded testosterone (creams, gels) used in vulvar tissue health and HSDD literature. MATRIX keys on testosterone.
Local oestrogen for genitourinary syndrome of menopause. MATRIX keys on estradiol.
Generic mention covering local oestrogen formulations (cream, ring, tablet). MATRIX keys on estradiol; conjugated estrogens (Premarin) would be a separate entry if it ever appears in Whel data.
Systemic hormone replacement therapy in menopause. MATRIX keys on estradiol; conjugated equine estrogens (Premarin) would be a separate entry.
Native fetal oestrogen used in combined contraceptives (Nextstellis); E4 is the standard biochemical abbreviation. MATRIX keys on estetrol.
T3 thyroid hormone. Triiodothyronine is the chemical name; liothyronine is the INN/USAN. MATRIX keys on liothyronine.
Ovulation induction in PCOS and unexplained infertility. Citrate is the dispensed salt; MATRIX keys on clomiphene (canonical name in MATRIX drug-list is Clomifene, the INN spelling).
Proactive entry. Frequent in European literature on dysmenorrhoea and chronic pelvic pain. Added so future Whel rows using the INN spelling resolve to the same DrugBank entry as acetaminophen.
Proactive entry. Frequent in UK and European contraceptive literature. Norethindrone is the USAN; norethisterone is the INN. Same compound.
Proactive entry. Frequent in UK and Australian vulvodynia / pelvic-pain literature. MATRIX keys on lidocaine.

Proactive entry. Frusemide is the older BAN/INN spelling still common in UK and Australian literature; furosemide is the USAN. Same compound. Relevant where diuretics appear in PMDD or perimenopause symptom-management lists.
